concept.headline: Rust variables are immutable by default — add mut to allow change.
concept.explanation: When you write `let x = 5;`, Rust creates an immutable binding. The value 5 is stored in memory and the name x points to it — but you can never write a new value into that slot. If you need to change a variable, declare it with `let mut`. This default immutability is Rust's first line of defence against bugs: if a value should never change, the compiler guarantees it never does.
concept.syntax:
let x = 5;          // immutable
let mut y = 10;     // mutable
const MAX: u32 = 100; // compile-time constant

// Shadowing — new binding, same name
let x = x + 1;     // x is now 6
keyPoints: let x = 5 creates an immutable binding — compiler error if you try to reassign
let mut x = 5 allows x = new_value later in the same scope
const must have a type annotation and a compile-time-known value
Shadowing (re-declaring with let) creates a fresh binding — the old one is gone

demo.intro: Watch Rust bind an immutable value, then a mutable one, and see what changes in memory at each step.

explanation.why: let without mut creates a read-only slot; the compiler rejects any future write to x
explanation.intent: Establish a named, stable reference to the value 5
explanation.misconception: Immutable does not mean the value is stored differently — it means the compiler prevents re-binding the name

explanation.why: let mut reserves a writable slot — the value can be overwritten later in this scope
explanation.intent: Create a variable we plan to update during execution
explanation.misconception: mut does not change the type — y is still i32; it only changes whether reassignment is allowed

explanation.why: y was declared mut, so the compiler allows this write
explanation.intent: Update the mutable slot with a new value
explanation.misconception: This would be a compile error if y was declared without mut — Rust enforces immutability at compile time, not runtime

explanation.why: println! reads both values out of memory and formats them into a string
explanation.intent: Observe the final state of both bindings
explanation.misconception: println! does not consume the variables — they are still accessible after this line

prediction.explanation: x starts at 3, then x = x + 2 evaluates 3 + 2 = 5 and stores 5 back into x. println! outputs the current value: 5.

explanation.afterDemo: You saw two slots created in memory: x (read-only) and y (writable). When y = 20 ran, the 10 was overwritten with 20. x never changed — and if you had tried to write to x, the compiler would have stopped you before the program even ran. This is Rust's compile-time safety at work.
keyTakeaways: Immutability is the default — you must opt in to mutability with mut.
The compiler, not the runtime, enforces immutability.
Shadowing with let creates a completely new binding, allowing a type change too.

practice.prompt: Declare an immutable variable `a` with value 7. Declare a mutable variable `b` with value 3. Add a to b (b = b + a). Print b.
practice.hint: Use let a = 7; and let mut b = 3; then b = b + a; and println!("{}", b);

feedback.successMessage: Great! You correctly used an immutable binding for a and a mutable one for b, then updated and printed b.
retryHints: Make sure a is declared without mut — it should never change.
b must be declared with let mut so you can reassign it.
The expression b = b + a reads a and b, adds them, and stores the result back in b.
println!("{}", b) prints the current value of b — expected output is 10.

summary.title: Variables & Mutability — Complete
takeaways: let creates an immutable binding by default.
let mut allows reassignment within the same scope.
Rust enforces immutability at compile time, not at runtime.
